retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- History and Physical -- Staphylococcus aureus
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who present with nausea and vomiting as their predominant symptoms have likely ingested a preformed enterotoxin like that generated by Staphylococcus aureus or Bacillus cereus . In addition, chemical irritants or viral gastroenteritis also present with nausea and vomiting as the initial or prevailing symptoms. Ingestion of the preformed toxin causes symptoms within 30 minutes to 8 hours of ingestion and resolves within 24 hours. Foods not cooked after handling or stored at unsafe temperatures are at high risk for Staphylococcus aureus contamination. The primary source of contamination is food handlers who carry Staphylococcus aureus in their noses or hands or through respiratory secretions.

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Staphylococcal enterotoxin-induced food poisoning: Staphylococcus aureus causes very similar symptoms with sudden onset nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain within 2 hours after eating contaminated food. These patients, however, may develop fever, while scombroid patients do not. Also, patients with scombroid typically have flushing or rash, while Staphylococcus food poisoning does not.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Gastroenteritis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Enterotoxins like C lostridium perfringens and Bacillus cereus cause watery diarrhea within 8 to 16 hours of ingestion. Viral infections are associated with cramps and watery diarrhea after 16 to 48 hours. Some pathogens are related to bloody diarrhea. These agents can cause symptoms up to 120 hours after ingestion. Some agents can cause manifestations within minutes, for example, copper, shellfish toxins, tin, and other seafood (pufferfish) through tetrodotoxin or scombroid with histamine release.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Spores -- Clinical Significance -- Food Poisoning
retrieved_docs.content: Endospores causing food poisoning include: Bacillus cereus Clostridium perfringes

retrieved_docs.title: Bacillus Cereus -- History and Physical -- Gastrointestinal Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: For emetic-type illnesses, symptoms include nausea and vomiting, similar to the presentation associated with Staphylococcus aureus food-borne illness. Diarrhea may be present in some individuals as well. Symptom onset is generally within 30 minutes to 6 hours of consuming rice or starchy foods left at room temperature, even after being reheated. This illness is due to the presence of cereulide, an ionophoric low-molecular-weight dodecadepsipeptide that is pH-stable and resistant to heat and proteases. Symptoms for both types usually resolve within 24 hours after onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Review of Bioterrorism -- Issues of Concern -- Symptoms and Signs
retrieved_docs.content: In human beings, botulism is usually caused by serotypes A, B, E, and F. There are six recognized forms of botulism: foodborne, infant, intestinal, wound, iatrogenic, and inhalational, characterized by different routes of exposure and incubations periods. The lethal dose for an adult is 0.7 to 0.9 micrograms of inhaled toxin to 70 micrograms of ingested toxin. Food-borne botulism is usually caused by ingestion of home-preserved food and presents typically within the first 4 hours. Infant botulism occurs by the ingestion of spores in infants one to six months in age and has an incubation period ranging from five to 30 days. Intestinal botulism is caused by the ingestion of spores by children over the age of years and adults and has a variable incubation period. Wound botulism is caused by the germination of spores in a wound and has an incubation period of 1 to 2 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Scombroid poisoning results from eating fish that have been improperly stored. The primary toxic agent implicated in scombroid toxicity is histidine, which is normally found in the dark meat of fish, and breaks down to histamine. In temperatures warmer than 4 degrees Celsius, the fish undergo bacterial overgrowth and subsequently convert histidine to histamine, resulting in very high levels of histamine. This activity reviews the presentation of scombroid toxicity, the types of fish and bacteria most commonly involved, and the management of this condition. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team management of scombroid toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Gastroenteritis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical manifestations will be related to the pathogen. Ingestion of toxins ( S. aureus ) can lead to a rapid onset of nausea and vomiting within 6 hours of digestion. Patients may have fever, cramps, and diarrhea within 8 to 72 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The poisonings occurring after Swiss cheese ingestion have been due to bacterial contamination of the raw milk prior to processing. The pathophysiology of the conversion of histidine to histamine remains the same as in histamine toxicity of fish.

retrieved_docs.title: Botulism -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: In one study, the results showed the incubation time for wound botulism was 4 to 14 days from the time of spore introduction. [27] An extensive systematic review by Chatham-Stephens et al identified 233 studies and 171 patients with botulism with available data on foodborne and wound botulism clinical features from 1935 to 2015. [28] The median incubation period was 1 day, and the shortest reported was 2 hours. Inhalational botulism is rare and is likely to be associated with accidental exposure from the environment (such as oral ingestion or laboratory inhalation) or intentional malintent. [29] Exposed individuals may receive a lethal dose following 2 nanograms of BoNT per kilogram weight. Exposure can also occur via direct inoculation in iatrogenic botulism.

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Scombroid poisoning results from eating contaminated fish that have been improperly stored after being caught. The primary toxic agent implicated in scombroid poisoning is histidine (normally found in dark fish meat), which breaks down to histamine. In temperatures warmer than 4 C, the fish undergo bacterial overgrowth and subsequently convert histidine to histamine. In properly stored fish, histamine is normally less than 0.1 mg per 100 g, but histamine levels in contaminated fish are at least 20 to 50 mg per 100 g of fish. The responsible bacteria are most commonly Escherichia coli , Vibrio , Proteus , Klebsiella , Clostridium , Salmonella , and Shigella . [5] [2] [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium botulinum Infection(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of botulism can occur suddenly and usually appear 12 to 36 hours after exposure to the BoNT, although they can range from a few hours to a few days. [3] An extensive systematic review by Chatham-Stephens et al identified 233 studies and 171 patients with botulism with available data on foodborne and wound botulism clinical features from 1935 to 2015. [22] The median incubation period was 1 day, and the shortest reported was 2 hours. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike most food poisoning, scombroid poisoning is not secondary to ingestion of a virus or bacterium, but the source of symptoms is chemical. Histidine exists in multiple different types of fish, especially dark meat fish, and at air temperatures above 4 C (40 F), it is converted to histamine via the enzyme histidine decarboxylase. Enteric bacteria such as Escherichia coli , Vibrio , Proteus , Klebsiella , Clostridium , Salmonella , and Shigella are responsible for this conversion. Normal cooking temperatures do not destroy histamine. Histamine is then ingested and leads to an allergic-like reaction by degranulation of mast cells. This degranulation mechanism is similar to that seen in allergy-mediated histamine response. [5] [1] [7] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Botulism -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: After BoNT exposure, the time to symptom onset depends upon the toxin dose and the relevant absorption kinetics. For foodborne botulism, symptoms typically appear within 12 to 72 hours of ingesting contaminated food, although onset times from as little as 2 hours to as long as 8 days have been recorded. [15] [25] For infectious (ie, wound and infant) botulism, the onset depends on the duration of spore exposure, the time required for germination, and the rate at which the resulting colonies produce enough BoNT to induce symptoms, which varies significantly based on bacterial species, toxin serotype, and the patient’s age and immunological status. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Review of Bioterrorism -- Issues of Concern -- Symptoms and Signs
retrieved_docs.content: In Typhoid fever, the incubation period ranges between seven to 14 days after initial inoculation. Following this, patients can present with fever and abdominal symptoms, including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. A stepladder pattern of fever and relative bradycardia is classically associated with typhoid and enteric fever. [20] [22] Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly can develop during disease progression. Rose spots, which are blanching erythematous maculopapular rashes consisting of lesions that are two to four mm in diameter, can develop on the abdomen and chest. [20] Other Salmonella infections can present with bacteremia or as focal infections, including gastroenteritis, meningitis, osteomyelitis, and urinary tract infections. [21]

Clinical findings such as “bacterial infection; unspecified site” and “المزرعة إيجابية” (positive culture) are classified under bacterial infection, underscoring the importance of these antibiotics in both prophylaxis and treatment while highlighting the need for careful adverse‑effect monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 620
retrieved_docs.content: overlapping nature of toxicological classifications and the importance of recognizing both acute and delayed effects, as well as intentional versus unintentional exposure, in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 911
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning caused by valproate is a toxic condition in which the medication valproate acts as the causative agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2600
retrieved_docs.content: Yersinia pestis, the bacterial pathogen responsible for plague, is associated with infections of the skin and subcutaneous tissues, manifesting as lesions such as buboes that reflect the organism’s ability to invade and proliferate within these superficial layers. Recognizing this link is important for clinicians when evaluating patients with cutaneous or subcutaneous swellings, especially in endemic areas or after exposure risks, to ensure prompt diagnosis, appropriate antimicrobial therapy, and infection‑control measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2266
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs is classified as a late effect of poisoning due to a drug, medicinal, or biological substance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: to ensure accurate dosing, detect contamination, and guide clinical decision‑making. Collectively, this framework integrates safety monitoring, regulatory oversight, and analytical scrutiny to optimize the safe use of these diverse pharmacologic agents and to mitigate risks associated with poisoning and toxicity in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2277
retrieved_docs.content: for specific counter‑measures to manage toxic exposures. The inclusion of protective substances among potentially poisonous agents highlights that any chemical, regardless of intended safety purpose, may become harmful at inappropriate doses or under certain physiological conditions. Recognizing these classifications supports clinicians, toxicologists and public‑health professionals in selecting appropriate drugs, monitoring therapy, promptly identifying toxic exposures, implementing decontamination and treatment strategies, and educating patients about risks associated with both prescription medications and seemingly benign chemicals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Shock is classified as صدمة إنتانيّة, indicating that the condition known as shock falls under the category of septic shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: Chemically induced processes are recognized as applicable qualifiers for a range of medical conditions, including تمزق (tear), اعتلال الكلية السكري (diabetic nephropathy), فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), and التسمم الوشيقي (septicemia). In addition, the qualifier “chemically induced” itself may be further specified by the presence of خلل الحركة المتأخر (delayed movement disorder) or by فطار غزوي (aggressive feeding). This framework highlights that certain pathologies can arise from chemical triggers and that the nature of those chemical inductions can be refined by additional clinical features, aiding precise characterization and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin, a broad‑spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, may be contraindicated or its use may be avoided in the presence of an infection caused by unicellular organisms (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), underscoring the need to consider the specific type of pathogen when selecting antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1588
retrieved_docs.content: Other and unspecified environmental and accidental causes is classified as a child category under the broader parent grouping “e codes: other specified; nec,” indicating that this specific set of environmental and accidental etiologies is organized within the external cause (E‑code) taxonomy as an “other specified; not elsewhere classified” subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 279
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxokobalamin, a hydroxylated form of vitamin B12, may be used as a therapeutic agent for the treatment of poisoning (التسمم). By binding toxic substances such as cyanide, hydroxokobalamin facilitates their conversion into non‑toxic metabolites that can be safely eliminated from the body, making it a valuable option in the management of acute toxic exposures. Its potential use in poisoning underscores the importance of recognizing specific antidotes in emergency and critical care settings.
